content: MG treatment includes symptomatic therapy and immunotherapy. Symptomatic: pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) 30-120mg every 3-6 hours—first-line for all patients; side effects include muscarinic symptoms (salivation, lacrimation, diarrhea, bradycardia) treated with glycopyrrolate. Immunotherapy for inadequate pyridostigmine response: (1) Corticosteroids (prednisone)—rapid onset but long-term toxicity; start low and titrate to avoid transient worsening. (2) Non-steroidal immunosuppressants: azathioprine (slow onset 6-12 months, check TPMT), mycophenolate mofetil, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, methotrexate. (3) Rapid therapies for exacerbations/crisis: IVIG (2g/kg over 2-5 days) or plasma exchange (5 exchanges)—temporary bridge to slower immunosuppressants. (4) Biologics: eculizumab/ravulizumab (complement C5 inhibitors, AChR+ refractory MG), efgartigimod (FcRn blocker, reduces IgG), rituximab (especially MuSK-MG). Thymectomy: indicated for thymoma (all ages); recommended for AChR+ generalized MG age <65 even without thymoma (MGTX trial showed benefit). Crisis management: ICU care, intubation if FVC <20mL/kg or NIF <-30cmH2O, IVIG/PLEX, treat triggers, hold pyridostigmine when intubated (increases secretions).
